_meta.authoringNotes: WHO 2022 classification. Tefferi A et al. Blood 2013 diagnostic criteria. Marchioli R et al. N Engl J Med 2013 (ECLAP) cardiovascular events data. JAK2 V617F from Kralovics R et al. Blood 2005. Cytoreductive therapy from CRIMPATH and Sokal trials.

h1Title: Polycythemia Vera: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis and Treatment
heroOverview: Polycythemia vera (PV) is a clonal myeloproliferative neoplasm driven by a JAK2 gene mutation (JAK2 V617F in >95% of cases, or JAK2 exon 12 mutations in the remainder) that causes uncontrolled proliferation of erythroid, granulocytic, and megakaryocytic precursors in the bone marrow. The hallmark is an elevated red cell mass — hemoglobin and hematocrit above age/sex-adjusted thresholds — creating hyperviscosity of the blood that substantially increases the risk of both thrombotic events (stroke, myocardial infarction, deep vein thrombosis, portal vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism) and hemorrhagic complications (from platelet dysfunction). PV is uncommon, with an incidence of 0.5-2.0 per 100,000 persons annually in high-income countries, median age at diagnosis 60-65 years, and male predominance (approximately 1.5:1). The disease follows a chronic, incurable course; while allogeneic stem cell transplantation can cure selected patients, most are managed with phlebotomy, low-dose aspirin, and cytoreductive agents (hydroxyurea first-line, with interferon-alpha, ruxolitinib, and busulfan as alternatives) for life. The ECLAP trial demonstrated that phlebotomy to hematocrit <45% in men and <42% in women significantly reduces cardiovascular death and major thrombosis compared with no phlebotomy. PV transforms to post-polycythemic myelofibrosis in 10-15% of patients at 10 years, and to acute myeloid leukemia in 2-5% at 10 years — the most feared complication.

keyStats.prevalence: Incidence 0.5-2.0 per 100,000 annually; prevalence approximately 20-30 per 100,000 in Western populations
keyStats.demographics: Median age at diagnosis 60-65 years; male predominance 1.5:1; rare before age 40 except in women (possible hormone-related)
keyStats.avgAge: Median diagnosis 63 years; median survival 14+ years with modern management
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 100,000-150,000 patients in the US and EU combined; higher prevalence in Ashkenazi Jewish populations

definition: Polycythemia vera (ICD-10: D45) is a JAK2-mutant clonal hematopoietic stem cell disorder classified under myeloproliferative neoplasms in the WHO 2022 scheme. The JAK2 V617F mutation (valine to phenylalanine substitution at codon 617 of Janus kinase 2) is present in approximately 95% of PV patients; the remaining ~5% carry JAK2 exon 12 mutations (N542Efs*15, E543Dfs*11, and others) that produce an erythropoietin-independent JAK-STAT activation. This constitutive kinase activation drives erythropoiesis independent of erythropoietin (EPO), explaining the autonomous increase in red cell production without the expected EPO suppression. Granulopoiesis and thrombopoiesis are also EPO-independent and increased. The clonal stem cell origin was established by clonality studies using X-chromosome inactivation patterns in female patients. PV is distinguished from secondary erythrocytosis (EPO-driven from hypoxia, renal disease, or EPO-secreting tumors) by the presence of the JAK2 mutation, suppression of serum EPO, and the presence of cytogenetic abnormalities (chromosome 20q deletion, trisomy 8, trisomy 9) that confirm clonality. PV is managed by hematologists, with input from vascular medicine (thrombosis risk management), dermatology (pruritus), and transplant specialists for eligible candidates.

typesClassification.description: The predominant form (>95% of cases). JAK2 V617F allele burden correlates with phenotype: higher burden associated with more pronounced leukocytosis, higher thrombosis risk, more pruritus, and higher transformation rates to myelofibrosis and AML. Quantified by digital PCR or quantitative PCR.

A distinct PV phenotype with isolated erythrocytosis, often younger patients, low or normal EPO, and JAK2 exon 12 mutations. Presents with isolated hemoglobin elevation without leukocytosis or thrombocytosis. Must be distinguished from primary familial and congenital polycythemia and high-affinity hemoglobin.

The fibrotic transformation phase of PV, occurring in 10-15% of PV patients at 10 years and increasing to 20-25% at 20 years. Characterized by splenomegaly, cytopenias (anemia from marrow fibrosis and hypersplenism), leukoerythroblastosis, and constitutional symptoms (fatigue, night sweats, early satiety from splenomegaly). Diagnosis requires WHO bone marrow morphology criteria.

A clinically aggressive subtype of PV with normal or near-normal hemoglobin but elevated red cell mass. Presents with thrombocytosis, leukocytosis, and low serum EPO — a pattern that mimics essential thrombocythemia. Associated with higher thrombotic risk than overt PV despite lower hemoglobin values. Requires JAK2 mutation and iron deficiency masking erythrocytosis.

primarySymptoms: Erythromelalgia — burning pain, warmth, and redness of the palms and soles that is relieved by cold water immersion; caused by platelet-mediated microvascular occlusion
Aquagenic pruritus — itching after contact with warm water (hot shower, bath) that can be severe and disabling; present in 40-65% of PV patients, often worse at night
Headache and blurred vision (plethora-related hyperviscosity) — early morning headache, dizziness, and visual disturbances from sluggish retinal blood flow
Fatigue and weakness — from hyperviscosity reducing oxygen delivery despite elevated hemoglobin; also from occult iron deficiency (consumed by erythrocytosis)
Plethora — ruddy complexion, conjunctival injection, and visible venous distension — the classic physical finding that gives the disease its name
Splenomegaly — palpable left upper quadrant mass in 30-50% of PV patients; causes early satiety, left shoulder discomfort, and abdominal fullness
Nosebleeds, bleeding gums, and easy bruising — platelet dysfunction in PV despite often elevated platelet counts
Tinnitus and non-specific auditory symptoms — hyperviscosity effects on cochlear microcirculation
Erythema and livedo reticularis on extremities — dusky, mottled skin from sluggish flow in cutaneous vessels
B symptoms (unexplained weight loss, night sweats, fever) — in advanced disease or pre-fibrotic transformation
earlyWarningSigns: Unexplained thrombosis (stroke, DVT, PE, MI) in a middle-aged or older adult — particularly if accompanied by erythrocytosis or thrombocytosis on CBC
Splenomegaly with erythrocytosis — the combination strongly suggests a myeloproliferative neoplasm including PV
Aquagenic pruritus — itching specifically after warm water contact is highly suggestive of PV and rarely seen in other conditions
Progressive fatigue and erythromelalgia — combination of microvascular symptoms with elevated hemoglobin/hematocrit on routine testing
Thrombocytosis combined with iron deficiency anemia — masked PV can present this way when iron is depleted by continuous erythrocytosis
emergencySigns: Acute stroke symptoms (facial droop, arm weakness, speech difficulty) — hyperviscosity from PV is a major stroke risk factor; PV accounts for ~10% of Budd-Chiari syndrome cases
Acute chest pain with dyspnea suggesting MI or PE — thrombosis at any site (arterial or venous) is the primary life-threatening complication of PV
Budd-Chiari syndrome (hepatic vein thrombosis) — PV is one of the most common underlying myeloproliferative neoplasms; presents with abdominal pain, ascites, hepatomegaly, and jaundice
Massive upper GI bleeding from peptic ulcer disease — PV patients have hypersecretory gastric mucosa and platelet dysfunction, making erosive GI disease dangerous
Acute mesenteric ischemia — visceral vessel thrombosis in hyperviscosity state

causes.description: The JAK2 V617F gain-of-function mutation causes constitutive activation of the JAK-STAT signaling pathway, rendering hematopoietic cells EPO-independent. Present in >95% of PV cases. The mutation occurs in the hematopoietic stem cell.

A group of mutations in JAK2 exon 12 produce PV with isolated erythrocytosis without leukocytosis or thrombocytosis. Found in ~3-5% of PV patients. Mechanism: localized activation of JAK2's JH2 pseudokinase domain.
PV originates from a mutated hematopoietic stem cell that has a proliferative advantage over normal stem cells. X-chromosome inactivation studies in female PV patients confirm the clonal nature.
PV patients often have elevated serum vitamin B12 from increased transcobalamin III (produced by granulocytes). This is a secondary finding, not a cause.

riskFactors.description: Age is the strongest predictor of thrombotic events in PV. Patients over 60 have 3-5 fold higher thrombosis rates compared with younger patients and are the primary indication for cytoreductive therapy.
Patients with a prior thrombotic event have a 5-10 fold increased risk of recurrence. This is the strongest indication for aggressive cytoreduction and anticoagulation.
High mutant allele burden correlates with increased thrombosis risk, more pronounced myeloproliferation, and higher rates of transformation to myelofibrosis and AML.
Leukocytosis in PV is independently associated with increased thrombotic risk and higher mortality. It reflects more aggressive clonal expansion.
Smoking induces hypoxia, stimulating erythropoiesis and worsening hyperviscosity. Also increases baseline thrombosis risk that compounds PV's already elevated thrombotic tendency.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 60; median age at diagnosis 63 years; rare before age 40 except in women of childbearing age
Slight male predominance (1.5:1) in most populations; more balanced in Mediterranean countries
Slightly higher prevalence in Ashkenazi Jewish populations; rare in all ethnic groups
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing polycythemia vera (PV) begins with confirming true erythrocytosis — a hematocrit persistently above the sex-adjusted upper limit, typically above 49% in men and above 48% in women. Iron stores must be assessed before interpreting hemoglobin, as phlebotomy-induced iron deficiency can mask true erythrocyte mass. Serum erythropoietin (EPO) is the key next step: a low or inappropriately normal EPO in the setting of erythrocytosis points strongly toward primary myeloproliferative neoplasm, whereas an elevated EPO suggests secondary polycythemia from hypoxia (sleep apnea, COPD, high altitude), renal disease, or EPO-secreting tumors. If EPO is low or normal, the JAK2 V617F mutation is tested — present in over 95% of PV patients. If negative, JAK2 exon 12 mutation testing identifies the minority of cases. Bone marrow aspiration and biopsy are required by WHO 2016 diagnostic criteria for PV, and typically show hypercellularity, panmyelosis, and prominent erythroid hyperplasia. Trilinear hyperplasia with megakaryocyte pleomorphism is characteristic. Reticulin fibrosis, if present, suggests transformation toward myelofibrosis. Cytogenetic studies and comprehensive mutation panels (ASXL1, TET2, DNMT3A, IDH1/2) help refine prognosis and transformation risk — ASXL1 and IDH1/2 mutations are associated with higher risk of transformation to myelofibrosis or acute leukemia.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count with RBC indices and iron studies
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identify erythrocytosis; RBC mass elevation confirms true polycythemia vs. relative polycythemia from dehydration. Low serum ferritin suggests iron depletion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Hemoglobin >165 g/L men, >160 g/L women is the first alert. Serum ferritin, transferrin saturation, and iron help interpret low hemoglobin values.

diagnosticTests.test: JAK2 V617F mutation testing (quantitative PCR or digital PCR)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirm clonality. Present in ~95% of PV. Quantification of V617F allele burden predicts phenotype and risk.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Peripheral blood test. Turnaround 1-3 days. Quantitative result is important — higher burden (>50%) indicates higher thrombosis risk.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Minor criterion for PV diagnosis. Low EPO (<3.3 mU/mL) supports PV; normal-to-elevated EPO suggests secondary polycythemia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood test. Low EPO in PV reflects negative feedback from expanded red cell mass suppressing renal EPO production.

diagnosticTests.test: Bone marrow aspirate and trephine biopsy
diagnosticTests.purpose: Major criterion for definitive PV diagnosis. Assesses cellularity, megakaryocyte morphology (large mature forms in PV vs. hypolobated in ET), and reticulin fibrosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Iliac crest biopsy under local anesthesia. Results 3-5 days. Required for diagnosis in JAK2-negative cases and for risk stratification.

diagnosticTests.test: Overnight oximetry or arterial blood gas
diagnosticTests.purpose: Exclude hypoxia-driven secondary erythrocytosis. Sleep study if overnight oximetry shows desaturations.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Exclude COPD, sleep apnea, high altitude as causes of secondary polycythemia before committing to PV therapy.

treatmentOverview: PV management pursues two simultaneous goals: reducing thrombotic risk through hematocrit control, and managing symptomatic burden. Phlebotomy is the first-line intervention — target hematocrit below 45% in men and below 42% in women. The CYCVT study demonstrated that achieving these targets reduced thrombotic events from 14.2 to 5.5 per 100 patient-years. Low-dose aspirin (75–100 mg daily) is added for all patients without specific contraindications. For higher-risk patients (age above 60 or prior thrombosis), cytoreductive therapy is mandatory: hydroxyurea is the standard first-line agent, typically starting at 500–1000 mg daily with blood count monitoring. For hydroxyurea-resistant or intolerant patients — defined as inadequate hematocrit control or poor symptom response after 3 months at maximum tolerated dose — ruxolitinib, a JAK1/2 inhibitor, is the preferred second-line agent and has demonstrated superior reduction in splenomegaly, symptom burden, and all-cause mortality in the RESPONSE trials. Interferon-alpha is an alternative especially for younger patients, given its disease-modifying potential and safety profile. Management of aquagenic pruritus after bathing requires specific strategies: antihistamines, JAK inhibitors, phlebotomy optimization, and phototherapy. Iron deficiency induced by phlebotomy should not be replaced until hematocrit is controlled — iron supplementation otherwise drives additional erythrocytosis. Patient education on early signs of thrombosis and the importance of adherence to phlebotomy schedules is essential.

medicalTreatments.name: Phlebotomy (therapeutic red cell apheresis)
medicalTreatments.description: The cornerstone of PV management. Target hematocrit <45% in men, <42% in women reduces thrombosis risk dramatically per ECLAP data. Initial phlebotomy 300-500 mL every other day until target hematocrit achieved. Iron supplementation usually not needed (iron deficiency is often therapeutically useful in PV to limit erythrocytosis).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ECLAP trial: phlebotomy to target hematocrit reduced CV death and major thrombosis by 60% vs no phlebotomy. Symptom improvement (reduced headache, dyspnea, plethora) within days of hematocrit reduction.
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: Phlebotomy every 2-4 weeks initially, then frequency adjusted to maintain target hematocrit. Lifelong. No drug resistance develops.

medicalTreatments.name: Low-dose aspirin (75-100 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Aspirin at 75-100 mg/day in PV prevents arterial thrombotic events by inhibiting platelet aggregation. Particularly effective for erythromelalgia symptoms (burning pain in extremities). High-dose aspirin worsens bleeding risk without additional antithrombotic benefit.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Low-dose aspirin reduces arterial thrombosis risk in PV (RR ~0.6). Combined with phlebotomy, this is the most effective thrombotic risk reduction strategy available.
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: Lifelong in all PV patients without major bleeding contraindications. Withheld if platelet count >1 million and active bleeding present.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydroxyurea (HU)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line cytoreductive agent for high-risk PV patients. HU is a ribonucleotide reductase inhibitor that suppresses bone marrow proliferation across all lineages, reducing hemoglobin, WBC, and platelet counts. Achieves hematocrit control in 75-80% of patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cytoreductive response in 75-80%. Reduces thrombosis risk by 60-70% in high-risk patients. Takes 2-6 weeks for hematocrit effect. Side effects include myelosuppression (neutropenia), oral and leg ulcers, and rare skin toxicity.
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: Starting dose 500-1000 mg/day. Titrate to maintain WBC 3-8 x 10^9/L, platelets <400 x 10^9/L, and hematocrit target. Long-term HU in PV associated with a small increased AML risk (2-3% at 15 years) but this is considered acceptable given the thrombosis prevention benefit.

medicalTreatments.name: Ruxolitinib (JAK1/JAK2 inhibitor)
medicalTreatments.description: A targeted JAK-STAT pathway inhibitor approved for PV patients who are intolerant or resistant to hydroxyurea. Rapidly reduces spleen size, improves constitutional symptoms, and improves quality of life substantially. Approved based on RESPONSE trial.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: RESPONSE trial: 21% of HU-refractory/intolerant PV patients achieved hematocrit control vs 1% on best available therapy; 60% achieved spleen volume reduction. Symptom improvement in 80% within 12 weeks.
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: 10-20 mg twice daily, titrated based on platelet count. Thrombocytopenia is the dose-limiting toxicity. Must not be discontinued abruptly — taper over 2 weeks to avoid rebound splenomegaly and cytokine flare.

surgicalOptions.name: Allogeneic stem cell transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: The only potentially curative therapy for PV. Reserved for patients with high-risk disease — transformation to myelofibrosis with poor prognostic features, or to AML. The only procedure that can eradicate the JAK2-mutant clone.
surgicalOptions.whatToExpect: Myeloablative or reduced-intensity conditioning transplant from a matched sibling or unrelated donor. Cure rates 60-70% in eligible patients under 65. TRM 15-25% at experienced centers. Requires careful risk-benefit assessment given transplant morbidity.

alternativeTreatments.name: Interferon-alpha (IFN-α)
alternativeTreatments.description: A cytoreductive agent that suppresses myeloproliferation through immunomodulatory mechanisms rather than direct cytotoxicity. Preferred over HU in younger patients, in pregnancy planning, and in those with concern about long-term leukemogenicity. Pegylated IFN-alpha-2a (Pegasys) is commonly used.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Hematocrit response in 70-80%. May reduce JAK2 allele burden over time (unlike HU). Achieves molecular responses in some patients. Side effects include flu-like symptoms, depression, and autoimmune phenomena.
alternativeTreatments.typicalCourse: Peg-IFN-α-2a 45-90 mcg weekly subcutaneously. Takes 3-6 months for full effect. Excellent for women of childbearing potential with PV.

alternativeTreatments.name: Busulfan (alkylating agent)
alternativeTreatments.description: A DNA alkylating agent used in PV patients who are hydroxyurea-resistant or -intolerant and ruxolitinib-failed. Effective at controlling cell counts. Rarely used first-line due to known leukemogenic potential.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Effective in HU-resistant PV. Can achieve long-term remission in selected patients.
alternativeTreatments.typicalCourse: Starting 4-6 mg/day, titrated to maintain blood counts. Monitor for cytopenias, pulmonary fibrosis, and AML transformation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Radioactive phosphorus (P32)
alternativeTreatments.description: An older therapy using systemic radioisotope to suppress bone marrow. Achieves remissions lasting 1-3 years with single doses. No longer preferred due to leukemogenic potential and availability of better alternatives.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Good control of hematocrit in elderly patients who cannot take oral medications. Single dose effect lasts 1-3 years.
alternativeTreatments.typicalCourse: Single oral dose of P32 (typically 3-5 mCi). Repeat only if and when relapse occurs. Avoid in younger patients due to AML risk.

whySeeSpecialist: PV management is complex and requires a hematologist with myeloproliferative neoplasm expertise. Risk stratification (low vs high risk), choice of cytoreductive agent, management of complications (thrombosis, pruritus, splenomegaly), surveillance for transformation, and the difficult decision about transplantation all require specialist experience. The hematologist coordinates with vascular neurology for stroke prevention, dermatology for refractory pruritus, and transplant teams when transplant is being considered.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a hematologist with specific MPN expertise (not just general hematology). In India, major centers like Tata Memorial Hospital Mumbai, AIIMS Delhi, CMC Vellore, and NIMS Hyderabad have dedicated MPN programs. Ask whether the hematologist uses the ELN risk stratification, has experience with JAK inhibitor therapy, and follows patients with annual bone marrow assessment. Multidisciplinary MPN clinics offer best care.
hospitalCriteria: Hematology department with MPN/multiple myeloma/cell therapy expertise
Stem cell transplant capability for AML or myelofibrosis transformation cases
Interventional radiology for central line placement and therapeutic apheresis
Coagulation laboratory capable of thromboelastography and platelet function testing
Bone marrow transplant unit with laminar airflow and nursing expertise
keyFacilities: Therapeutic apheresis unit for red cell exchange/phlebotomy programs
JAK2 V617F allele burden quantitative PCR (reference lab acceptable)
Hematopathology with expertise in WHO 2022 bone marrow classification of MPN
Clinical trials unit with access to MPN-targeted therapies (ruxolitinib, interferon trials, novel JAK inhibitors)
Pain and symptom management service for refractory pruritus and erythromelalgia
Genetic counseling for patients with strong family history of MPN or thrombosis

costBreakdown.consultation: Hematologist consultation: ₹500-₹2,000. Ongoing monitoring every 3-6 months: ₹300-₹1,000 per visit.
costBreakdown.diagnosticTests: JAK2 V617F PCR: ₹3,000-₹8,000. Bone marrow biopsy: ₹3,000-₹10,000. CBC and iron studies: ₹500-₹1,500. Serum EPO: ₹800-₹2,000.
costBreakdown.medications: Hydroxyurea 500 mg × 30: ₹200-₹500/month. Ruxolitinib 5 mg × 60: ₹25,000-₹60,000/month (expensive but increasingly covered by insurance). Aspirin 75 mg × 30: ₹50/month.
costBreakdown.surgery: Allogeneic stem cell transplant: ₹15-₹30 lakhs ($18,000-$36,000) in India. Worth considering for high-risk transformation cases.

insuranceGuide: In India, PV treatment including hydroxyurea, ruxolitinib (recently added to NPCDCS formulary in some states), and interferon is covered under CGHS, ECHS, and many private insurers. Ayushman Bharat PMJAY covers hospitalization and transplant in eligible families. International travel for treatment is not covered.
financialAssistance: Novartis patient assistance programs for ruxolitinib in India. Generic hydroxyurea is inexpensive. MPN Research Foundation (mpnrf.org) offers patient resources and clinical trial information. Many hospital social work departments help with medication cost assistance.
preventionStrategies: Maintain hematocrit below target (<45% men, <42% women) through regular phlebotomy
Cytoreductive therapy (hydroxyurea) for high-risk patients (age >60 or prior thrombosis)
Avoid smoking — worsens hyperviscosity and thrombosis risk
Adequate hydration to avoid falsely elevated hematocrit from dehydration
lifestyleModifications: Regular aerobic exercise (30 min/day) — improves microcirculation and reduces thrombosis risk
Smoking cessation is mandatory — smoking compounds hyperviscosity and thrombosis
Avoid iron supplementation unless specifically indicated — iron deficiency limits erythrocytosis
Maintain healthy weight — obesity increases thrombosis risk in PV
Avoid dehydration (saunas, prolonged exercise without fluid replacement) which falsely elevates hematocrit

recommended: High-iron foods if iron deficient (red meat, lentils, spinach) — but many PV patients are iron replete from continuous phlebotomy
Omega-3 fatty acid rich foods (fatty fish 2x/week) — may have mild antithrombotic effect
Antioxidant-rich fruits and vegetables — may protect against AML transformation
Adequate hydration (2-3 liters/day) to maintain normal hematocrit
avoid: Iron supplements unless medically indicated — iron repletion worsens erythrocytosis in PV
High-dose vitamin C supplements — may increase iron absorption and oxidative stress
Decongestant medications containing phenylpropanolamine — can raise blood pressure
Smoking and alcohol — both worsen hyperviscosity and thrombosis risk

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise (walking, cycling, swimming 30 minutes most days) is beneficial in PV — improves endothelial function, reduces thrombosis risk, and maintains quality of life. Heavy resistance training with Valsalva maneuver should be avoided as it transiently raises blood pressure and may be risky with thrombocytosis. Avoid scuba diving and high-altitude climbing without pre-acclimatization as hypoxia triggers erythrocytosis. Exercise tolerance should be monitored — unexplained fatigue out of proportion to disease severity warrants evaluation for transformation to myelofibrosis.
dailyManagement: Check hemoglobin/hematocrit and platelet count every 1-3 months once stable
Take low-dose aspirin daily unless contraindicated by bleeding
Phlebotomy every 2-8 weeks depending on hematocrit trend and JAK2 burden
Report any new thrombosis symptoms immediately (leg swelling, chest pain, stroke symptoms)
Aquagenic pruritus management: cool showers, antihistamines at night, capsaicin cream topically, phototherapy (PUVA) for severe cases
Erythromelalgia: cool water immersion of affected extremities; low-dose aspirin is specifically effective
Annual bone marrow assessment to monitor for fibrotic transformation (new cytopenias, increasing splenomegaly, constitutional symptoms)
Watch for transformation symptoms: new anemia, weight loss, night sweats, increasing spleen size — report to hematologist
Avoid NSAIDs if platelet count >1 million (increased bleeding risk); use paracetamol instead for pain
prognosis: Modern PV management has dramatically improved outcomes. Median survival exceeds 14 years with risk-adapted treatment, and most patients die of age-related causes rather than PV complications. The ECLAP data showed that phlebotomy to target hematocrit plus low-dose aspirin reduced cardiovascular death by 60%. High-risk patients (age >60 or prior thrombosis) on hydroxyurea have thrombosis rates of 1-2% per year — comparable to low-risk patients off cytoreduction. Transformation to post-polycythemic myelofibrosis occurs in 10-15% at 10 years and 20-25% at 20 years. Transformation to AML, the most feared complication, occurs in 2-5% at 10 years and is higher in patients with adverse cytogenetics, very high JAK2 V617F burden, and prolonged hydroxyurea exposure.
recoveryTimeline: There is no cure for PV; treatment is lifelong. Phlebotomy produces immediate symptom relief (reduced headache, dyspnea, plethora) within days. Hydroxyurea takes 4-8 weeks for full effect. Ruxolitinib produces spleen and symptom improvement in 4-12 weeks. Interferon-alpha takes 3-6 months for hematocrit control. Surveillance for transformation is lifelong with annual CBC and symptom review, and bone marrow every 3-5 years.
complications: Thrombosis — arterial (stroke, MI) and venous (DVT, PE, portal vein, hepatic vein Budd-Chiari) — the primary cause of mortality and morbidity in PV
Post-polycythemic myelofibrosis — progressive marrow fibrosis with anemia, splenomegaly, constitutional symptoms, and cytopenias; occurs in 10-15% at 10 years
Acute myeloid leukemia transformation — occurs in 2-5% at 10 years; historically higher in patients exposed to alkylators and radioactive phosphorus; poor prognosis once established
Major hemorrhage — GI bleeding from peptic ulcer disease or platelet dysfunction, or intracranial hemorrhage; typically occurs with platelet counts >1 million or concurrent antiplatelet use
Splenic infarction or rupture — rare but acute emergency; massive splenomegaly in advanced PV can lead to splenic rupture after minor trauma

supportResources.name: MPN Research Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy organization funding research into PV, essential thrombocythemia, and myelofibrosis. Offers patient education, webinars, and clinical trial matching

supportResources.name: Leukemia & Lymphoma Society
supportResources.description: Free information services, support groups, and financial assistance for blood cancer patients including PV

supportResources.name: GINEFPV (French PV patient registry)
supportResources.description: European PV patient database providing epidemiological data and outcome information

confusedWithConditions.name: Secondary polycythemia (hypoxia-driven)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Secondary polycythemia from COPD, sleep apnea, or high altitude has normal or elevated serum EPO (not suppressed as in PV), is not associated with thrombocytosis or splenomegaly, and does not carry JAK2 mutation. Treatment targets the underlying cause, not phlebotomy alone.

confusedWithConditions.name: Essential thrombocythemia (ET)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ET has elevated platelets without erythrocytosis (or minimal). PV has erythrocytosis as the primary abnormality. Both are JAK2-mutant MPNs but have different management. Masked PV with normal hemoglobin but high JAK2 burden can be misdiagnosed as ET — iron studies and EPO level help distinguish.

confusedWithConditions.name: Primary familial and congenital polycythemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: An inherited EPO receptor mutation causing elevated RBC without JAK2 mutation or clonal markers. Benign course with no increased thrombosis risk. JAK2 testing negative and EPO level low (same as PV) — distinguishes from PV by absence of JAK2, absence of thrombocytosis/leukocytosis, and family history.

coOccurringConditions.name: Essential thrombocythemia (ET)
coOccurringConditions.reason: Both are JAK2-mutant MPNs and share biology; some patients present with overlapping features (ET with mild erythrocytosis or PV with marked thrombocytosis) making classification challenging

coOccurringConditions.name: Myelofibrosis
coOccurringConditions.reason: Post-polycythemic myelofibrosis is the fibrotic transformation phase of PV; represents disease progression rather than a separate condition

coOccurringConditions.name: Portal hypertension and Budd-Chiari syndrome
coOccurringConditions.reason: JAK2 mutation is the most common finding in Budd-Chiari syndrome cases; PV accounts for 10-15% of myeloproliferative neoplasm-driven hepatic vein thromboses

relatedConditions.name: Essential thrombocythemia
relatedConditions.relationship: Sister MPN driven by JAK2, CALR, or MPL mutations; overlapping biology and management principles

relatedConditions.name: Myelofibrosis
relatedConditions.relationship: The fibrotic transformation product of PV (post-polycythemic myelofibrosis) and primary myelofibrosis share clinical features

relatedConditions.name: Acute myeloid leukemia
relatedConditions.relationship: The most feared transformation of PV; JAK2 mutation persists in AML cells that arise from PV clone

relatedConditions.name: Secondary polycythemia
relatedConditions.relationship: Differential diagnosis for erythrocytosis; must be excluded before PV diagnosis is confirmed

faqs.question: What is polycythemia vera and how does it differ from having a high hemoglobin?
faqs.answer: Polycythemia vera (PV) is a clonal blood cancer where a mutated JAK2 gene causes the bone marrow to overproduce red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets. A high hemoglobin alone can be 'secondary' — caused by smoking, lung disease, sleep apnea, living at high altitude, or dehydration. PV is distinguished by the JAK2 mutation, suppression of erythropoietin hormone, bone marrow changes, and splenomegaly. Your hematologist will run specific tests to confirm which type you have.

faqs.question: What is the JAK2 mutation and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: The JAK2 V617F mutation is a change in the JAK2 gene that makes the JAK2 protein permanently 'on' — like a stuck accelerator pedal in a car. This causes blood cells to grow without the normal controls. JAK2 V617F is found in >95% of PV patients. Testing for it confirms PV is present (not just secondary erythrocytosis). The amount of JAK2 mutation (allele burden) also helps predict your thrombosis risk — higher burden means higher risk.

faqs.question: Is polycythemia vera curable?
faqs.answer: PV is not considered curable with current standard treatments (phlebotomy, aspirin, hydroxyurea). However, allogeneic stem cell transplantation can cure selected patients with high-risk disease or transformation to myelofibrosis/AML — this is the only potentially curative option but carries significant risks. For most patients, PV is managed as a chronic condition for decades with excellent quality of life and survival approaching normal age-matched populations with modern therapy.

faqs.question: How is PV treated and what are the main options?
faqs.answer: All PV patients get phlebotomy (blood removal) to keep hematocrit below target (<45% men, <42% women) and low-dose aspirin to prevent blood clots. High-risk patients (age >60 or prior blood clot) also get cytoreductive drugs: hydroxyurea is first-line, interferon-alpha is preferred in younger patients, and ruxolitinib is used when hydroxyurea doesn't work or causes side effects. Each treatment has specific benefits and risks that your hematologist will discuss with you.

faqs.question: What is the biggest risk of having PV?
faqs.answer: Blood clots (thrombosis) are the biggest risk in PV — affecting arteries (causing stroke, heart attack) and veins (causing DVT, PE, or Budd-Chiari syndrome in the liver). This is why keeping hematocrit controlled and using aspirin or cytoreductive drugs when indicated is so important. With modern treatment targeting hematocrit <45%, thrombosis rates drop dramatically.

faqs.question: What is aquagenic pruritus and why does it happen in PV?
faqs.answer: Aquagenic pruritus is intense itching specifically after contact with water — especially warm water like a shower or bath. It affects 40-65% of PV patients and can be severe and disabling, often worse at night. The exact mechanism isn't fully understood but likely involves mast cell degranulation and histamine release triggered by water contact. Treatments include antihistamines, low-dose aspirin (which specifically helps), topical capsaicin, UV therapy, and ruxolitinib.

faqs.question: Can I live a normal life with PV?
faqs.answer: Yes. With appropriate treatment (phlebotomy, aspirin, and cytoreductive drugs for high-risk patients), most PV patients have a near-normal quality of life and life expectancy. Median survival exceeds 14 years with modern management. Most patients continue working, exercising, and traveling normally. The key is consistent monitoring (CBC every 1-3 months), staying on aspirin, maintaining hematocrit targets, and reporting any new symptoms promptly.

faqs.question: Does PV turn into leukemia?
faqs.answer: PV can transform to acute myeloid leukemia (AML) in approximately 2-5% of patients at 10 years from diagnosis. This is the most serious complication of PV. The risk is higher in patients with adverse cytogenetics (chromosome abnormalities), very high JAK2 allele burden, and prolonged use of chemotherapy agents like busulfan or radioactive phosphorus. Regular blood count monitoring and bone marrow assessment help detect transformation early.

faqs.question: What is the difference between phlebotomy and donating blood?
faqs.answer: Therapeutic phlebotomy for PV aims to reduce hematocrit to a specific target (<45% in men, <42% in women) and is performed at a medical facility. Blood donation is for transfusion purposes and doesn't target a specific hematocrit level. However, many PV patients who meet criteria can also donate blood for transfusion purposes when their hematocrit is controlled. Discuss with your hematologist.

faqs.question: Is hydroxyurea safe long-term for PV?
faqs.answer: Hydroxyurea is the most widely used first-line cytoreductive agent for PV worldwide and has an excellent safety record over decades. Concerns about a possible increased AML risk (2-3% at 15 years vs. 1% in untreated PV) must be balanced against the substantial reduction in thrombosis risk — blood clots are a more immediate threat than slow AML transformation. For most high-risk PV patients, hydroxyurea provides significant net benefit.

faqs.question: Can women with PV have children?
faqs.answer: Yes, women with PV can have successful pregnancies with careful multidisciplinary management by a hematologist and high-risk obstetrician. Hydroxyurea is typically stopped before conception (with a 3-month washout) and interferon-alpha is used instead during pregnancy. Phlebotomy maintains hematocrit targets throughout pregnancy. Pregnancy outcomes are generally good with close monitoring, though risks of thrombosis and miscarriage are elevated.

faqs.question: What monitoring is needed for PV over time?
faqs.answer: PV requires lifelong monitoring. CBC with differential every 1-3 months to track hematocrit, WBC, and platelet trends. Annual review of symptoms (pruritus, splenomegaly, thrombosis). Bone marrow examination every 3-5 years to assess for fibrotic transformation. JAK2 V617F allele burden quantification annually or when clinical change is observed. Watch for new cytopenias (possible myelofibrosis transformation) or blast elevation (possible AML).
